query: Nose wheel shimmy during ground ops. Dampener serviced recently. What does the FSM landing gear troubleshooting say about shimmy?
fsm_probable_cause: Shimmy dampener low on fluid / Worn steering components / Tire flat-spotted
fsm_remedy: Service dampener / Replace worn parts / Replace tire
gus_diagnosis: The FSM troubleshooting points to several potential causes for nose wheel shimmy. We need to systematically check for mechanical play and wheel integrity. Begin by inspecting the nose wheel assembly for any obvious looseness or damage.
gus_reasoning: The FSM troubleshooting guide for the landing gear (Source 28, 34, 50, 51) identifies several probable causes for nose gear instability, including 'WHEEL BOUNCE EVIDENT EVEN ON SMOOTH SURFACE' and 'NOSE STRUT WILL NOT HOLD AIR PRESSURE,' which are directly related to nose wheel shimmy. Despite recent servicing of the dampener, a defect or improper service is still a possibility. However, other common mechanical issues such as wheel balance, loose torque links, loose wheel bearings, loose steering linkage, loose strut clamps, incorrect tire pressure, or a bent axle are also highly probable causes for shimmy. My initial approach will focus on verifying the most common and easily observable mechanical looseness and wheel condition.
gus_answer_paths: The nose wheel has excessive play when rocked side-to-side, or the tire pressure is incorrect.
The nose wheel torque links (Figure 5-12, Source 14) show visible looseness or wear at their attachment points.
The nose wheel steering rods or their connections to the steering arm (Figure 5-11, Source 2) have noticeable play.
The nose strut itself is loose in its attaching clamps to the firewall (Figure 5-6, Source 41), or the shimmy dampener still feels ineffective despite recent service.
